title: <div>In large simulations or worldbuilding (e.g. massive game worlds, VR metaver
content: <div>In large simulations or worldbuilding (e.g. massive game worlds, VR metaverses, or scientific simulations), multiple subsystems and creators contribute to one “world,” requiring rigorous naming and version control to avoid chaos: • Global Entity IDs: Complex simulations often assign each object or entity a global ID so it can be referenced unambiguously across modules. For instance, a city simulation might give every building or agent a unique number or UUID; a distributed physics sim might tag each data point with the simulation run ID and timestamp. This ensures that when different subsystems (rendering, AI, networking) talk about “Object 42,” they mean the same thing. Similarly, in a collaborative worldbuilding project, unique IDs for lore items or characters prevent duplicate entries. The use of GUIDs in Unity we saw is analogous – Unity ensures that a prefab named “Tree” in module A and another “Tree” in module B won’t clash, because under the hood they have different GUIDs unless intentionally unified. • Universal Scene Description (USD): A modern exemplar of unifying complex assets is Pixar’s USD (Universal Scene Description), now adopted industry-wide for 3D scenes and
context: From document: Transforming a Fragmented Digital Life into a Unified Canonical Architecture — chunk 9 (chunk 9/37)
